data.title: KSLV: Silver Exposure With High Income And Few Compromises
data.description:
Recently, I covered the Kurv Gold Enhanced Income ETF (KGLD) and gave it a buy rating, because I liked both its strategy and execution, something rare for me in the income ETF market. So it felt only natural, because I am bullish on silver going into next year, to cover KGLD's sister fund, the Kurv Silver Enhanced Income ETF (KSLV).

Much like KGLD, KSLV comes with this lofty goal right front and center on the Kurv ETFs website:

KSLV seeks to outperform price return of silver.

Here is the fund's progress toward that goal so far, but due to it being so new (inception is this past September), the jury is still out on whether it will be able to keep up moving forward.

Remember also, that I'm using total return here, which includes all dividends reinvested at the time of distribution. I use this to show the totality of the funds' returns, but most income investors, I assume, are trying to take the income, at least some of it. We remain competitive with the underlying asset in a bull market (which silver is currently in). At least some of the distributions must be reinvested.

Here's just the price action:

KSLV Holdings & Strategy

Price $35.50
Inception Sept. 29, 2025
Distribution Rate 21.27%
Dividend frequency Monthly
Expense Ratio 1.00%

KSLV's strategy is fairly straightforward: buy long silver exposure, then sell call options against that exposure. This trades some upside participation for high, immediate income, and is the same for basically all covered call funds.

Kurv takes a less straightforward approach to actually building the fund. Instead of buying an ETF like SLV, the most popular silver ETF, they build synthetic long positions with options to mimic the exposure of holding outright SLV shares while also being able to hold treasury bills as collateral. This sounds more complicated than its end result is, which is effectively the same exposure as just holding the SLV outright and T-bills.

It means that their holdings look like this:

Unlike many other cover call funds, KSLV takes an interesting approach to the trade-off between income and appreciation. While their upside is limited, they also buy excess long call options to add more silver exposure back into the mix. This means that the fund ends up looking almost like 120% long and 20% short, which should get us roughly to 100% exposure. This is why Kurv says that the goal is to keep up with silver, and why they're so close to doing so. It's not perfect, but it's done a good job so far.

It also adds a risk on the downside because that extra exposure can work against the fund as well. So while most covered call funds could be expected to outperform their uncovered counterparts in a bear market or sideways market, KSLV is likely to just keep up with Silver's losses and not have any sort of downside buffer due to the income, since that disparity is made up for in excess long exposure.

When we map out the full options spread as of time of writing (keep in mind that these things change day-to-day as Kurv trades them and maintains KSLV's exposure levels), it looks like this.

Note the nonlinear upside, especially toward the break-even point at $65 on SLV, but also, that there is no hard cap currently. It seems that we're also in a period where there is very little upside being sold. The 70C options in January are the only short call position and is greatly outnumbered by long positions. This is an active stance taken by Kurv, and one that investors should understand going into the fund. That they are able to modulate their short-term exposure and cut back on income if they believe that silver will continue to appreciate more. Their goal is outperformance and income, there's a balancing act that the managers undertake here.

KSLV Dividends & Taxes

Assuming that folks are invested in this fund for income, I felt it only right that we go over the few distributions that have been made so far. Currently, the fund has only made three, all of which have been either 50 or 55 cents/share. This gives KSLV a distribution rate of over 20%.

The most compelling part here is that options income, when filtered through the ETF vehicle to shareholders, is entirely classified as Return of Income ("RoC"), which is considered advantageous by investors. RoC income does not create a taxable event when it is distributed to shareholders, instead being counted against one's cost basis. This means that when distributions hit the accounts of KSLV shareholders, they get the money but do not have to pay taxes on it that year, per se. Instead, they can defer the taxes until their shares are sold. If one's cost basis hits zero, then all future distributions do create taxable events, but are taxed as long-term capital gains in the year that you receive them.

This is still more advantageous to investors than receiving the dividends as ordinary income and so makes these kinds of funds more desirable for taxable accounts.

According to Kurv ETF's latest 19-a-1 form which estimates the tax implications of distributions, 95% of KSLV's last distribution was attributed to RoC, and 5% to ordinary income.

The Silver Bull Thesis

Of course, all of this hinges on investors already having a bullish stance on silver. If one were to be expecting silver to fall, then KSLV is not a good option and should not be considered.

The silver bull thesis requires its own article to fully do it justice, but here are some basic points to consider:

Precious metals, namely gold and silver, are currently in a bull market. This has a lot to do with de-dollarization trends in the second and third worlds, where foreign central banks are turning to precious metals as an alternative to treasuries and dollars in their reserve holdings. In a recent article on gold, I highlighted a theory of mine about a future BRICS currency; I recommend giving it a full read to see the full thesis, but here is a key excerpt:

There just isn't enough gold for [the BRICS currency] to be entirely gold-backed. But if we added silver, platinum, and palladium to the mix, things start to check out a little more. Look at where these metals are mined [the BRICS+ countries are highlighted]. Of special note is the monopoly BRICS has on platinum and palladium mining.

In another positive note for silver, US banks have closed their short positions that started the year with. While non-US banks are still net short, they are at levels below their highs over the summer. It is interesting to see this divergence, and telling that some banks are now net long silver.

Outside the monetary thesis, silver has a quality that is not found in gold, in that it has key industrial uses. Advanced manufacturing is able to turn silver into a paste, which is critical for technologies like solar panels. In the past, this industrial use was seen as a limiting factor because silver's price tends not to be as based on monetary debasement as much as it is on industrial demand. In the past year, it's seen that this has changed, and silver has been speculated on in a very similar way to gold, despite my past fears of it being repressed by industrial dumping at high prices.

But high prices that were previous barriers have come and gone, and silver has set new highs regardless. Industrial silver might just become more expensive structurally, and that could raise the prices of some technologies -- but it is unlikely from my view to be a reason for silver to decline from here.

Conclusion

Ultimately, KSLV has impressed me, just like KGLD did, for its ability to keep up with the underlying asset while still distributing high income. While it is still lagging behind, it is only slight, and that trade-off seems far worth it for income investors as opposed to more traditionally constructed covered call funds. It does come with risks. If my silver bull thesis doesn't play out as expected because of industrial dumping or because of a structural shift back towards dollars and treasury bonds, then KSLV will not offer any solace for investors -- also, unlike traditionally constructed covered call funds, which can pad their downsides with the income.

For structural reasons around silver itself and because of KSLV's intelligent construction and so far -- it's still very new -- excellent execution, I am giving it a buy rating. Income investors interested in diversifying their portfolios into precious metals should consider more than just silver, and I recommend those interested in this fund also seek out KGLD.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: ICOP: Copper Great, But Iron Ore A Bit More Downside Exposed
data.description:
The iShares Copper and Metals Mining ETF (ICOP) is obviously significantly exposed to copper with exposures like Freeport-McMoRan (FCX), but also other mining majors like Anglo American (NGLOY) and BHP Group (BHP), which all have considerable copper exposure as well but also matched by exposure in other areas such as bulk commodities. This is the pattern with some of the other top holdings in the portfolio as well. But there are other considerations in terms of resources for this portfolio as well, like iron ore and, to some extent, gold. Copper seems unstoppable, and electrification efforts will take a long time, and there are mine outages as well as tariff speculation activities taking place that are helping the commodity. Gold has value in a more uncertain geopolitical and economic landscape. Iron ore is a bit more sensitive, as China is pressuring the majors to lower their prices coming into China and doing things to prevent as much speculative activity. Moreover, steel production is also iffy. Materials stocks are not generally that cheap anymore, reflected in the ICOP PE, mainly driven by copper. We aren't seeing a major angle here other than a continuation of known trends.

ICOP Breakdown

The 27.46x PE reflects the higher multiples on copper stocks, above the 36x level, driving the average PE up. The general situation with the commodities is the following:

  • Copper is having another strong year. On one hand, there is plenty of demand related to electrification, which is going to be a secular force as a lot of investment is still required, and on the other hand are supply considerations. In addition to renewed tariff-related speculation in the US, there are also outages at mines that are pressuring supply. The trend currently is that copper is being stockpiled in the US and that there is still a ways to go before warehouses are filled. A lot of the low-hanging fruit in terms of mining has been taken advantage of, and we are not yet seeing a major exploration drilling and sampling cycle to anticipate that, which means there could be some period of deficit.
  • Iron ore is a little bit more iffy, and it has to do with a spat between China and the major iron ore miners. The dynamics here are that China is a major steel producer and therefore needs to import a lot of iron ore, but the iron ore prices are not very low, despite weak trends—that is to say, declines—in steel production. There are some market reasons for this, such as lower freight prices pushing a restocking trend by China for iron ore, despite weaker steel end markets. China is beginning to impose rules on those selling iron ore to China to disincentivise speculative behaviours that may be raising iron ore prices from the port, and there could be more rules in development to try to pressure the iron ore producers when selling to China.
  • Gold is quite a positive story and has demonstrated itself as a good hedge against economic and geopolitical uncertainty this year, and with uncertainty in general continuing with respect to rate questions, cost of capital, and economic growth, we think there isn't too much gold downside.

Bottom Line

ICOP is not just copper. Some of the major exposures have iron ore, gold, and other commodities in the mix as well, such as Anglo American and BHP. So there's already almost 10% iron ore exposure just among the very top holdings. Then there is gold exposure among some of the copper miners further down the list. In addition to operational considerations, which can make correlations hazy, the fact that it's not all copper at ICOP is reflected in the somewhat lower correlations between copper index ETFs and the ICOP.

The valuation is driven by copper, though, and given that the copper trend is so well analysed, we struggle to imagine that there's an edge here—other than perhaps the possible movement into deficit if the market is not fully appreciating the low-hanging fruit concerns for supply, beyond tactical and speculative considerations around tariffs.

data.title: Nvidia: Poised To Unlock $6 Trillion In 2026
data.description:
Wall Street analysts believe that Nvidia (NVDA) is significantly undervalued, and I totally agree with it. The company's valuation multiples are poised to contract dramatically over the next few years as it keeps converting AI-powered revenue growth into aggressive profitability growth. Nvidia's conversion of accounting profits to free cash flow is also stellar, which helps in accumulating shareholders' wealth while increasing financial power to reinvest in growth. I continue buying the stock aggressively, and NVDA remains a no-brainer Strong Buy for me. NVDA is up by almost 8% since my September 19 call.

Reasons to continue buying NVDA

Nvidia's market cap is currently $4.6 trillion, which makes it the world's largest company. Apple (AAPL) is second with its $4 trillion market cap, which is 15% lower than Nvidia's. Considering that just one week is left before the end of 2025, the probability that Nvidia will end this year in the status of the world's largest company is extremely high. And Wall Street analysts believe that $4.6 trillion is extremely cheap, as they project a 33.7% upside potential. Multiplying Nvidia's current market cap by the projected upside ($4.6 trillion by 1.337) implies a fair value of $6.15 trillion.

On the one hand, the potential market cap of $6.15 trillion might sound unrealistic given the fact that Nvidia has not been able to maintain a $5 trillion milestone for an extended period. On the other hand, all these milestones are just psychological factors rather than fundamental ones. To understand whether something is probable, possible, or impossible, it is always better to look at the numbers. For growth companies, forward multiples are crucial numbers.

Without context, Nvidia's current fiscal year (FY2026) forward P/E is quite high at 40. On the other hand, the historical average is almost 46. Furthermore, the current P/E ratio of 40 is explained by aggressive growth expectations regarding the company's EPS. Nvidia's EPS is expected to more than double between FY2026 and FY2028, meaning that the P/E ratio is poised to shrink dramatically to below 20. To calculate my target price, I prefer to use the FY2027 EPS estimate from consensus (average opinion of more than 50 Wall Street analysts), which is $7.55. Even if we multiply the company's current forward P/E ratio of 40 (which is significantly lower than the historical average) by this forecasted EPS of $7.55, we get $302 per share. Nvidia's current share price is around $190, meaning that the stock is indeed substantially undervalued.

To achieve this target price, Nvidia needs to deliver a 61% YoY EPS growth, which will be challenging, but it is doable given a combination of bullish catalysts. First and foremost, cloud giants (AWS, Microsoft, and Google), together with Meta, are not slowing down in their aggressive AI infrastructure plans. All of them confirmed during their Q3 earnings calls that they will increase capital investments compared to 2025, which is a big tailwind for Nvidia, the company dominating the GPU space. I know that almost two months have passed since these giants released their Q3 earnings, and they might have changed their capex plans, which have not yet been announced.

However, several recent developments confirm that data center buildout plans are still aggressive. For example, just two days ago it was announced that Google is buying data center and energy infrastructure company Intersect for $4.75 billion. The fact that Google is ready to invest $5 billion in an energy infrastructure company means they expect energy demand from their data centers to grow aggressively. Microsoft recently announced that it will invest almost $18 billion in India to boost cloud and AI infrastructure over four years between 2026 and 2029. Amazon plans a massive investment of up to $50 billion to expand AI infrastructure for U.S. government agencies. We haven't seen fresh news about data center investments from Meta, but we know that the company is working on developing two new LLM models, 'Mango' and 'Avocado.' This is direct evidence that Meta will need computing infrastructure expansion because it will be impossible to build new LLMs out of thin air. Therefore, the largest generative AI players are showing no signs of cutting their AI infrastructure budgets for 2026, which is a structural tailwind for Nvidia.

Apart from highly likely soaring GPU demand from data centers in 2026, there is another rapidly growing area where Nvidia is poised to capitalize. This thriving end market is autonomous ride-hailing, or robotaxis. Competition in this field between giant players is poised to intensify in 2026. Waymo's geographic footprint continues expanding, and the number of rides has tripled in 2025 compared to 2024. I am skeptical about Tesla's prospects overall, but Elon Musk aggressively pushes the robotaxi narrative, and analysts from Morgan Stanley expect Tesla to have one million robotaxis by 2035. And there is currently another emerging player backed by a tech behemoth, Amazon's Zoox, which is moving towards commercialization. Since Nvidia does not only sell GPUs for robotaxi companies but also has a software platform, NVIDIA DRIVE AV, which is already deployed by Stellantis, Lucid, and Mercedes-Benz, the company is also extremely well positioned to capitalize on another secular trend.

Despite having two big secular tailwinds behind its back, the company continues working extremely hard to find and develop new long-term growth catalysts. Nvidia's R&D budget has more than quadrupled over the last five years, which did not hinder operating margin expansion from 28% in FY2021 to almost 60% at the moment (TTM basis). And the company continues to be extremely strong in terms of converting accounting profits into free cash flow, which grew from $3.8 billion in FY2021 to $53 billion TTM, growth by almost 14 times.

Being such a cash-printing machine positions Nvidia firmly to invest in nascent industries with multi-trillion potential, like robotics and quantum computing. These two areas are significantly further from commercialization than robotaxis. However, analysts of the World Economic Forum (WEF) see great potential for both robotics and quantum computing to significantly boost productivity for industrial operations, manufacturing, and supply chain management. As we see, Nvidia's management continues planting seeds across the most powerful secular trends of the modern world.

Reasons to avoid NVDA

There are several companies watching Nvidia's exponential growth over the past five years, and I am confident that they want to bite a large piece of this highly profitable GPU pie. Apart from traditional rivals like AMD and Intel, there are other prominent players joining the race. Google's TPUs are also used for high-performance AI infrastructure, and the search giant is gaining traction in this domain. Other cloud giants, Amazon and Microsoft, are also working on their in-house AI chips. Even if they develop these chips for internal use only, it is still a risk for Nvidia because they might cut Blackwell or Rubin orders because their in-house chips will give comparable performance and efficiency.

Besides American tech giants developing their AI chips, there is also China. There is not so much information about China's AI chips, but there are local giants like Huawei and Alibaba that are also working hard to develop cutting-edge processors. If China develops a processor that is comparable to Nvidia's flagship offerings and is cheaper, it might not only undermine Nvidia's prospects in China but internationally as well because of the U.S. tariff wars against its large trade partners.

The verdict

Nvidia looks extremely undervalued, as a very conservative forward P/E ratio assumption multiplied by the FY2027 EPS estimates yields a fair price of $302, while the current price is below $200. This gap looks absolutely unfair given Nvidia's firm positioning to capitalize on the continued data center boom and highly likely accelerated robotaxi deployment in 2026. Furthermore, Nvidia is investing aggressively in new, potentially explosive areas like robotics and quantum computing.

data.title: Nvidia: What Should Investors Make Of The Groq Deal (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Last night, it was reported that Nvidia (NVDA) plans to buy the majority of Groq's assets, a leader in AI inference chips called LPUs, for $20 billion in cash.

A few months ago, I was early to note Nvidia's investment in OpenAI was a red flag, and indeed, investor perception of the AI landscape has been far more volatile and cautious since.

Today, I think I'll be early to call out that Nvidia's plan to acquire Groq's assets is a testament to the fact that GPUs aren't the perfect solution for every AI use case.

NVIDIA Plans To Acquire Groq's Assets & Personnel

Yesterday, CNBC reported that Nvidia plans to acquire Groq for a total consideration of roughly $20 billion in cash. Groq then affirmed the news, with a bit of nuance regarding the structure of the deal:

Today, Groq announced that it has entered into a non-exclusive licensing agreement with Nvidia for Groq’s inference technology. The agreement reflects a shared focus on expanding access to high-performance, low cost inference.

As part of this agreement, Jonathan Ross, Groq’s Founder, Sunny Madra, Groq’s President, and other members of the Groq team will join Nvidia to help advance and scale the licensed technology.

Groq will continue to operate as an independent company with Simon Edwards stepping into the role of Chief Executive Officer.

GroqCloud will continue to operate without interruption.

The structure of the deal reminds me a lot of the acquihires done by Microsoft (MSFT) with Inflection, Google (GOOG) with Charcter.ai, or Meta (META) with Scale AI.

These are types of deals that seek to avoid a prolonged process, especially on the regulatory front. While this could be viewed cynically, and it could be argued that this deal shouldn't go through antitrust, there's also a pragmatic justification here. The AI landscape is evolving so quickly that even a few months of interruption could be an insurmountable setback.

So, there you have it, a 'Non-Exclusive' license agreement that essentially strips Groq of the majority of its assets, including its key-man leaders in Sunny Madra and Jonathan Ross, and sends them to Nvidia.

Groq was valued at $6.9 billion in its latest funding round in September, which means Nvidia is paying more than triple the valuation over just a few months. Also, GroqCloud is not included in the deal, so the increase in valuation is even larger.

That said, Nvidia sits on a boat-load of cash and prints it at a faster pace than it knows what do with it. Also, if Nvidia's last large acquisition of Mellanox is any telling, then this $20 billion price would look minuscule in a few years.

What Does Groq Do? What Are LPUs? And What Is Nvidia's Angle?

Founded nine years ago by a team of engineers that include Jonathan Ross, one of the creators of Google's TPUs, Groq develops and builds custom chips that are tailor-made for AI inference.

These Language Processing Units, or LPUs, are designed to deliver extremely fast and low-cost inference, which is increasingly becoming the dominant use-case for AI.

NVIDIA's GPUs are best-in-class for a multitude of use cases, but they aren't necessarily the perfect solution for every individual use case. For instance, Nvidia's GPUs are good for non-AI data center compute, AI training, gaming, AI inference, and much more. While this makes the GPUs very valuable, it also makes them too expensive for certain use-cases, and arguably not best-in-class in others.

There are some rumors that Nvidia's Blackwells are part of OpenAI's struggles to complete its next frontier training run, and there are more and more deals for Google TPUs, which are cheaper and specifically designed for AI training and inference.

Groq's LPUs take it up a notch. Not only do they focus solely on AI, but they focus solely on inference, an area that is considered a bit of a weak-point for Nvidia. After all, the cost of its GPUs is so high that not many companies can afford to scale their AI inference needs, which are growing exponentially, with them.

So, what is Nvidia's angle here? I think it's simple. They want to cement their leadership in the inference space by integrating Groq's capabilities into the Nvidia stack. In the hands of Nvidia, with its access to TSMC capacity and customer relationships, Groq could quickly become a multi-billion-dollar business.

From CNBC:

In an email to employees that was obtained by CNBC, Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang said the agreement will expand Nvidia’s capabilities.

“We plan to integrate Groq’s low-latency processors into the NVIDIA AI factory architecture, extending the platform to serve an even broader range of AI inference and real-time workloads,” Huang wrote.

NVIDIA's Results Show GPUs Are In A Class Of Their Own

According to the reports, Groq was on track to reach $500 million in revenue this year, and based on estimates, Google's plans to sell TPUs externally could drive as much as $13 billion in revenue by 2027.

Meanwhile, Nvidia's data center revenues reached $51.2 billion last quarter alone, adding a 'Google TPU Business' in a quarter, after growing 25% Q/Q, and 67% Y/Y. The rest of the business is also growing rapidly, with consolidated revenue growth of 22% Q/Q and 62% Y/Y.

Despite higher prices from TSMC, an accelerated innovation cycle, and a complete halt of its high-margin China business, Nvidia maintains margins that are unheard of for a so-called hardware company.

Gross margins dropped 140 bps Y/Y, for the reasons we listed, but were still very strong at 73.6%. Meanwhile, revenue is growing much faster than operating expenses, as operational leverage offsets the entirety of the gross margin decline. As a result, operating income increased 62%.

Looking ahead, Nvidia expects to generate $65 billion in sales in Q4, reflecting 14% growth Q/Q and 65% growth Y/Y, which would mark an acceleration from Q3. Gross margins are expected to revert to 75%, and operating income should come to $44 billion.

Valuation Reflects Caution, Which Means It's Time To Buy

I was previously bearish on Nvidia for two main reasons. One, there was still uncertainty about next year's capex from hyperscalers, and the market priced the stock like there was none. Two, some of its actions, I thought, signaled concerns over looming weakness.

With the stock nearly 10% lower than its all-time highs, and with an increased level of market scrutiny on the AI space, I argue that expectations are now far more realistic. In fact, they are likely too low.

Consensus sees Nvidia growing revenues by 50% in calendar 2026 (noted as the fiscal period that ends in January 2027 in the picture), reaching $320 billion. In Q4, Nvidia is likely going to deliver $67+ billion in sales, with a typical beat, which means it's run-rating at $270 billion. For it to reach the current estimate, it needs to deliver Q/Q growth of 7%, which would be a sharp slowdown from the current 14%.

Meanwhile, at $188 per share, Nvidia trades at 25 times calendar 2026 estimates. Right now, CY26 estimates are shown as 'Forward 1Y', but after next quarter, they will be shown as 'Forward'. As you can see, a 25x multiple would be a trough multiple for Nvidia.

In my view, Nvidia could easily trade at 25 times CY27 estimates for $9.68 EPS by CY26 end, which takes me to a price target of $245 per share, reflecting 30% upside.

Conclusion

NVIDIA's move to acquire a fast-growing inference leader in Groq should help it fend off threats from use case-specific chipmakers. It could also be an accelerator of growth, as Nvidia's supply chain and customer relationships are far superior.

After a few months of treading water, Nvidia's valuation no longer reflects extremely high expectations. In fact, it likely reflects expectations that are too low.

As such, I upgrade Nvidia to 'Buy', with a price target of $245 per share.

data.title: Equinor: Share Price Pullback Makes It A High Dividend-Yielding Investment Opportunity
data.description:
Investment thesis.

Europe's natural gas inventories have been trending below the five-year average for the entire year. We are currently about 10% below those levels. I started buying Equinor (EQNR) shares at the start of the year, in response, given that it is Europe's largest provider of natural gas. Despite the fundamentals suggesting that there should have been a sustained increase in natural gas prices this year, the European spot markets headed in the opposite direction. As a result, Equinor's share price went roughly nowhere this year; however, it continued to pay its generous dividend, which makes the longer-than-expected wait for the fundamentals to kick in less frustrating. I decided to add to my Equinor stock position on the recent share price decline that happened in tandem with the unexpected, and arguably unexplainable, decline in Europe's natural gas spot prices.

Maintaining my buy position.

I last covered Equinor in early September, where I explained the favorable fundamentals as Europe was headed into heating season. The fundamentals continued to evolve favorably, as inventories continue to trend about 10% below the five-year average. However, the natural gas market did not react as expected. I continue to maintain a buy position because I believe that fundamentals always get to have a say in the end, even if not right away.

Equinor's solid financial results underpin its generous dividend.

There have been some suggestions that Equinor's generous dividend of just under 8% currently may not be sustainable.

As of right now, its dividend/share is $1.81, while its forward EPS is $2.60, as the chart above shows. I believe this to be a sustainable dividend outlay situation, since it is less than the company's earnings.

Another measure of dividend sustainability is its cash flow from operating activities as of the latest quarter. For the first nine months of the year, it saw an increase in cash flows after tax compared with the corresponding period in 2024 of 7%, to $14.67 billion. So far, Equinor has paid just under $3.9 billion in dividends for the first three quarters of the year. It also spent $5.53 billion on share buybacks.

Net income came in at $3.74 billion for the first nine months of the year, which is a decrease of 45% compared with the previous year. Low oil & gas prices played a significant role in this decline. Based on oil & gas price trends so far this quarter, we can expect another quarter of poor results for Equinor to wrap up the year.

Rising output may coincide with oil & gas price rebound.

Equinor has been increasing oil & gas production as well as its electricity generation output.

Output growth can help bring in more revenues and even profits, assuming that the rise in production was not achieved at the expense of profitability. Ultimately, increasing production can only do so much for improving Equinor's financial results. Oil & gas market trends will have a far greater impact than modest gains in output. If, however, both factors go in its favor, it can be a potent combination in terms of boosting its performance.

As the chart shows, Europe, Equinor's largest natural gas market, is facing low storage levels. It is a trend that lasted the entire year, yet there has been no market reaction to this reality to date. In fact, the TTF spot price went in the opposite direction since the start of the heating season, with a price decline of roughly 12%. Given that inventories are currently about 10% below the 5-year average, a relatively minor weather event this winter could cause natural gas prices in Europe to rise significantly. Natural gas prices are likely to remain elevated next year if we see a further deterioration in Europe's energy security situation in the next few months.

Equinor also has a sizable exposure to the oil market, which is currently not trending in favor of producers. The likes of the EIA & IEA are forecasting that it will not trend in favor of producers next year either. In other words, we are in a prolonged global oil market surplus situation that is forecast to last. OPEC, on the other hand, reports that in Q3, there was only a slight 0.4 mb/d global surplus in output, as opposed to the EIA estimating a roughly 2.5 mb/d glut in the same period.

As I have been stating for some months now, I see reasons to doubt institutional estimates and forecasting when it comes to the global oil market. The current oil market surplus situation was created by the OPEC+ group's restoration of oil output that they cut in prior years, which amounted to over 2 mb/d this year. The roughly 2 mb/d gap between OPEC & the EIA when it comes to the Q3 oil market balance signals that they have become agenda-driven. There is no way to know for certain whether the OPEC estimate or the EIA estimate for Q3 is more accurate. We should, by now, benefit from actual data, so there should not be such a sizable gap between them.

My take is that the current surplus is significantly less than the EIA estimate, but perhaps a bit larger than the OPEC estimate, so it is more than likely somewhere in between. Furthermore, the EIA estimate for OPEC spare capacity of over 4 mb/d is probably pure fiction. We have historical precedent suggesting that those numbers tend to be inflated. If we think back to the 2007-2008 period, when the Saudis & OPEC were called upon to stem the oil price surge that ended in an all-time high of nearly $150/barrel, they failed to produce that spare capacity.

OPEC+ most likely spent most of its real spare capacity this year, with very little left to add going forward. There are few prospects for oil output growth elsewhere around the world, so starting next year, I see a trend of oil market tightening. My guestimate is that we may potentially enter a prolonged period of oil supply shortfalls by the end of next year, assuming that global demand continues to grow at around 1 mb/d every year.

Equinor's unique market position versus peers.

If the European natural gas market continues to tighten going into next year, which is very much weather-dependent, while the global oil market also starts to tighten, Equinor could potentially see a double boost to its revenues, profits, and share price as a result of those improved results. However, even in the absence of both positive scenarios working out as I expect them to next year, Equinor could potentially thrive by having just one of the two markets trending in its favor.

To help visualize the different dynamics that Equinor's business profile offers versus other industry peers, I picked Suncor (SU), which is mostly exposed to the oil market, and Cheniere (LNG), which is exposed to the US natural gas market on the cost side, and to the European spot price market on the revenues and profits side.

Suncor, which produces mostly crude oil, derived products and has a marketing segment as well, did very well this year, considering the price of WTI crude declined by almost 20% YTD. It is a reflection of the broader oil industry, which managed to find a higher degree of resilience in the face of an unfavorable market. Equinor, which is exposed to the oil market and also had the added burden of declining natural gas prices in Europe, saw its share price perform significantly worse than Suncor's stock price.

Cheniere has been negatively impacted by higher natural gas costs on the US market, and lower LNG prices in the European & Asian markets. The European TTF spot price is down 1/3 in the past year. US natural gas prices are up about 17%, making for more expensive inputs for its LNG production. The inferior returns that investors experienced relative to Equinor have to do with Cheniere's results being squeezed from multiple sides, as well as the fact that, unlike Equinor, Cheniere does not have any exposure to oil and related products and services. Both companies are heavily tied to the market trends of natural gas and LNG, which helped to weigh both of them down, but Cheniere is a pure play on European & Asian natural gas trends, while Equinor does have the oil segment, providing a higher degree of diversification.

It is important to understand the difference between what it means to bet on energy companies with various business profiles. Cheniere could potentially outperform Equinor next year and beyond in the event that European natural gas prices rally, but oil prices stay low. It is especially the case if US natural gas prices were to go in the opposite direction and decline even as European prices move higher. Suncor could potentially outperform Equinor if oil prices rally but European natural gas prices do not. Equinor could potentially outperform both if energy prices rise across the board. It is especially the case when the effect of the dividend is included, which is currently more generous than Suncor's or Cheniere's. With a forward P/E ratio of 8.8, versus 13 for Suncor and 11 for Cheniere, Equinor also seems to have more upward potential in this regard.

Risks to my thesis.

My continued buy-and-hold thesis rests on the assumption that Equinor will be able to continue paying a generous dividend for the foreseeable future. There is always a risk that Equinor will not be able to sustain its generous dividend in the future. Oil prices could decline further from here, putting pressure on earnings. Europe's natural gas market could potentially experience a sudden shift in its dynamics if, for instance, peace breaks out and the EU unexpectedly decides to resume sizable Russian natural gas imports.

I do not believe that there are very high odds of a Russia-EU rapprochement in the foreseeable future. Oil prices cannot go much lower from here for a sustained period, because a growing number of oil-producing companies and regions cannot break even at current prices. The decline in US drilling activities this year is perhaps the most obvious market signal that suggests the current oil price level is not sustainable.

Conclusions.

I started building a position in Equinor stock based on what I see as long-term fundamentals going in its favor, and continued buying incrementally throughout the year. At this point, it became my second-largest position in the energy industry, surpassed only by Suncor. At first, I expected the continued positive fundamentals, namely the low natural gas inventory levels in Europe, to produce a rally in this stock, but the fundamentals don't always drive markets in the shorter term.

As I wait for fundamentals to kick in and drive this stock higher, I cannot help not to view this investment increasingly as an opportunity to build a long-term dividend-paying position. Even after the double taxation issue related to Norway's existing laws is included, the dividend yield that is near 8% currently is still generous. I do intend to take profits once the fundamentals kick in, and Equinor's share price will respond accordingly, but I will most likely maintain a sizable position in this stock, even if it does rally. Equinor yields significant dividend income, which, for now, I see no reason not to keep as a core part of my portfolio.

data.title: Innovative Aerosystems: High Growth Projections For High Reward
data.description:
Innovative Aerosystems (ISSC) is setting its sights on substantial growth over the coming years through organic reinvestment in advancing cockpit automation and through acquisitions. With management aiming to achieve $250 million in net revenue with an adjusted EBITDA margin of 25-30% by 2029, investor excitement has pushed the stock back near recent highs to 14.73x EV/aEBITDA. Given the robust outlook and growing market for aircraft modernization, I am recommending ISSC shares with a Buy rating with a price target of $24.58/share at 10.88x eFY28 EV/aEBITDA.

Given the recent run-up in the share price, I recommend investors take some precaution as shares may gyrate or pull back in the near term.

Innovative Aerosystems Operations

Innovative Aerosystems is setting its sights on the next phase of organizational growth as it enters eFY26 with the aim to drive profitable growth with a capital-disciplined approach. Overall, management’s 2029 target for Innovative Aerosystems is to grow revenue by 3x to $250 million with an adjusted EBITDA margin of 25-30%, aligned with the current margin. Revenue going forward will largely tie in high single-digit organic growth paired with M&A ambitions to expand its product offerings. As part of its growth strategy, management is planning to reinvest in the engineering and sales teams to drive innovation and enhance its go-to-market strategy. Innovative Aerosystems closed Q4’25 with $27 million in new orders for a book-to-bill ratio of 1.21x, resulting in backlog growth to $77 million, up from $72 million in Q3’25.

This in part will be led by scaling its F-16 program production and potentially other military aircraft programs at its Exton facility.

Accordingly, Innovative Aerosystems completed all recertifications of its digital flight control computer and is expecting to complete the recertification of its programmable display generator in the next month. Once recertified, Innovative Aerosystems should be well positioned to drive significant production growth as the firm’s backlog for F-16 products expands. As part of the program, Innovative Aerosystems should begin production at its Exton facility, inclusive of product line assembly, which should improve the cost structure of the business going forward.

Though Innovative Aerosystems has modest exposure to the defense industry, its low-cost, locally manufactured flight systems could potentially be an appealing offering for aircraft modernization. In 2024, the Department of Defense announced a $10b+ multi-year aircraft modernization budget in coordination with the Government of Japan. Given that Innovative Aerosystems products are essentially modular systems, ramping up production for larger defense contracts can be feasible and potentially help drive growth to management’s $250 million 2029 target.

Accordingly, the global military aircraft modernization & retrofit market was estimated at $62.6b in 2024 and is expected to grow at a 3.4% CAGR from 2025-2033 to $84.8b. As a result of the slow growth rate, Innovative Aerosystems will take on new market share in order to achieve its growth target.

Another innovation the firm plans to release in 2027 is the Liberty Flight Deck, which features an open-architecture avionics suite designed for passenger & cargo planes, business jets, and military aircraft. The platform includes touch displays, dual flight management systems, autothrottle, synthetic vision, and mission integration capabilities. In November 2025, JEKTA announced a collaboration with Innovative Aerosystems to integrate the Liberty Flight Deck into its PHA-ZE 100 amphibious aircraft. As of Q4’25, Innovative Aerosystems is in negotiations with 2-3 customers for the Liberty Flight Deck.

Another growth driver Innovative Aerosystems plans to launch is autonomous flight for the business jet market on its UMS2 platform, which integrates AI into the cockpit for enhanced automation as part of the Liberty cockpit. The program has been tested on the Pilatus PC-24 and is expected to be delivered beginning in June 2026. Accordingly, the Liberty Flight Deck program is expected to go into production beginning in 2030-2031 on the OEM side, whereas aftermarket is expected to begin in 2027.

Innovative Aerosystems Financial Position

Management set an ambitious target of reaching $250 million in net revenue by 2029, growing at a 31% CAGR. With the expectation of high single-digit organic growth, much of the growth will likely be achieved through M&A activity.

As part of its growth strategy, Innovative Aerosystems is looking to execute bolt-on acquisitions to vertically integrate the aerospace supply chain, focusing on additional product lines and potentially expanding its OEM business. To drive M&A, Innovative Aerosystems brought in Richard Silfen as an independent director. Silfen has a long history of working in lower middle market transactions on the legal side of operations.

In Q4’25, Innovative Aerosystems expanded its committed credit facility by $65 million to $100 million with $25 million options for a total of $125 million, providing significant flexibility to reinvest and engage in M&A growth. At the close of Q4’25, Innovative Aerosystems had $2.7 million in cash & equivalents and $24 million in debt on the balance sheet for a leverage ratio of 0.86x net debt/aEBITDA; Innovative Aerosystems had $75 million available under its existing credit facility, $30 million as part of its revolver and $45 million under its Delayed Draw Term Loan facility.

Given management’s growth ambitions, I’m making certain assumptions within my financial model that may result from inorganic growth, such as increased debt outstanding for financing deals as well as increased operating expenses. Looking ahead to eQ1’26, I’m forecasting Innovative Aerosystems to generate $19.3 million in net revenue with a diluted EPS of $0.17/share.

Risks Related to Innovative Aerosystems

Bull Case

Innovative Aerosystems is operating in a growing market with strong secular tailwinds across multiple classes of aircraft. With a growing need for military aircraft modernization, Innovative Aerosystems could be in an appealing position to take on market share covering additional programs. In addition to this, the firm’s next-generation cockpit could be a major growth catalyst for commercial and private jets.

Bear Case

Management’s ambitious $250 million revenue target for 2029 may result in investor skepticism if incremental growth isn’t realized. Though much of the growth may be driven by M&A, I believe the initial excitement following Q4’25 can quickly fade if the roadmap becomes unclear.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

ISSC shares have experienced a significant run-up in recent weeks following management’s strong outlook post-Q4’25 earnings. Though shares aren’t quite trading at the top end of their historical trading range near roughly 17.43x EV/aEBITDA, the premium has increased significantly following the massive share price rally.

Taking into consideration the growing market and the potential for Innovative Aerosystems to take on more market share in defense and commercial & private jets, shares could sustain a higher premium as part of an improving secular trend. Given management’s robust outlook for the firm, I believe shares may reflect incremental improvements in achieving the $250 million revenue target. Placing a value on shares, I believe ISSC shares should be priced at $24.58/share at 10.88x eFY28 EV/aEBITDA; I am recommending ISSC shares with a Buy rating.

Using the model: the valuation table above references my financial forecast in the firm’s “financial position” section and ties it to the stock’s historical trading premiums. The trading premium array is derived through the normal operating cycle, with the blue-sky scenario being the stock’s peak multiple and the gray-sky scenario being the lowest point. The target multiple aims for the midpoint, or the most likely trading range for the company’s stock. The trading multiples from there are set to a probability factor based on the likelihood of the stock trading at that premium based on its historical presence. From there, the trading multiple is tied to the probability factor to derive its relative market capitalization and relative multiple.

data.title: Permian Resources: The E&P You Need In Your Portfolio
data.description:
Introduction

It has been a while since I last reviewed one of my favourite E&Ps on the market: Permian Resources. Since my last analysis in early March, the company has posted a total return of over 19%, while WTI has declined from over $70 a barrel to $58, aligning with my initial thesis that PR will surmount volatility.

As a little reminder, Permian Resources (PR) is the second-largest pure-play E&P company in the Permian Basin. It focuses on developing high-return oil and liquids-rich natural gas properties in the area and is the best class in aligning shareholders and management.

Current dynamics

I’ll first start with the latest earnings release to grasp the bigger picture of what has been going on at PR. Q3 came in relatively mixed, with a small miss on revenue of $10MM, amounting to a total of $1.32B, which is still an 8.2% Y/Y growth despite lower commodity prices. Yet, on the bottom line, PR posted a beat of 8 cents on its Q3 Non-GAAP EPS of $0.37. Furthermore, Adj. FCF came in at $469MM, which is the highest in the company’s history and was a 50% increase from the previous quarter, thanks to higher production and aggressive cost-cutting.

Diving deeper, PR has simply over-delivered operationally thanks to the successful integration of acquisitions as well as highly efficient drilling. Volume reached 410,225 Boe/d, above what the street was expecting, thanks to a major driver that was the Haley project, a 17-well development. It was quite innovative as it used proprietary precision wellbore placement technology. Following this, PR keeps reducing its cost base, as it dropped to $725 per lateral foot, an 11% Y/Y reduction. On this trend, lease operating expenses also fell to $5.07 Boe, which is one of the lowest in the industry. On the balance sheet side, Net-Debt-to-EBITDAX fell to 0.8x, leaving the company in the safe zone to have an investment-grade credit rating, with Fitch upgrading the company to a BBB- grade, all the while prices have been in the doldrums.

Following this, the company has updated its guidance for the rest of the year, with an increase of 3,000 barrels per day compared to the midpoint of the full-year outlook, and mostly the production target was increased while keeping the capital budget unchanged. Moreover, thanks to new agreements regarding natural gas, the company will be able to add around $100MM in extra FCF by selling around 330MM cubic feet of gas per day while avoiding the low natural gas prices by selling at Gulf Coast LNG prices.

Outside of earnings that were, in my opinion, extremely good considering the current dynamics of the O&G industry, PR has also completed a slew of bolt-on acquisitions through Q3 and Q4, totaling 250 transactions for $180MM, with most of these coming in Eddy and Lea Counties in New Mexico. The company is actively buying small “gaps” where they can drill 3-mile laterals instead of 1.5-mile ones, doubling the amount of oil produced from a single wellbore, drastically reducing the break-even price.

Outside of these small bolt-ons, as APA was cleaning its portfolio from the acquisition of Callon Petroleum, PR scooped in to buy a block of assets in the Northern Delaware Basin in the same counties in New Mexico for $608MM. This will add 12,000 Boe/d in production, of which 45% is high-margin crude oil, while the inventory is standing at 100 high-quality drilling locations with an average breakeven price of roughly $30/bbl WTI. Moreover, the company acquired 8,700 royalty acres, in which PR will essentially pay itself a portion of the revenue on its own wells, increasing its profit margins on every barrel produced. I personally think that the deal was really smart on PR’s side, as it adds to its presence in the region and allows for 3-mile lateral drilling.

Permian Resources also just announced yesterday that it will transition to a regular C-Corp, which will only have a single class of publicly traded shares, removing the previous dual-class system. I believe that the goal of this would be that the company is positioning itself to be more wanted by certain institutional investors as well as index funds. It will also be easier to be included in major indices.

Valuation

Despite Permian having some of the best operations and costs in the industry, it is still relatively well priced, at around the middle of its peers, with its P/E Non-GAAP of 9.92, below companies like Diamondback and Cottera. The company is also generating high levels of FCF despite current WTI prices, and this translates to a current FCF yield of 6.9%, the highest over the past 3 years. Permian is also touted as one of the most shareholder-friendly companies in the energy industry, thanks to its high current yield of 4.26% that has grown substantially since 2023. Furthermore, the company is buying back stock at a consistent rate and has around $920MM remaining under the latest buyback authorization. Even the management is heavily invested in the firm, with the two co-CEOs receiving 100% of their compensation in equity.

The growth metrics are slightly skewed due to the merger back in 2022 that created the modern-day Permian Resources, but looking ahead, I’m bullish on the company’s strategy of industrialized efficiency. Meaning that the company’s goal is to maximize the value of every dollar spent through technological precision and not just drilling more wells. Currently, the company holds one of the deepest high-quality inventories in the Delaware Basin, around 15 years of core drilling locations remaining. PR has stated that they expect 3-5% annual growth while keeping CapEx flat, thanks to super laterals, which is simply rare in the industry right now.

To highlight how profitable the company is and the leaps it has taken, management has stated that they can generate the same levels of cash at $60/bbl as they did a year ago at $75/bbl. This directly translates into the highest EBITDA and operating margins among its peers at 74.61% and 38.4%, respectively. Company-wide, the breakeven per barrel is estimated to be in the low $40s, which still leaves a significant room even with the current prices on the market. With the new credit rating, PR will also have less interest expenses, boosting its bottom-line margin. Moreover, the company has relatively good capital efficiency metrics, with a ROCE of 10.1% and an ROE of 8.9%.

Risks

Even if the company is highly efficient, it is still almost entirely tied to the price of WTI and regional natural gas. Many analysts are projecting WTI prices to continue to drop in 2026 due mainly to a global oversupply. PR is still profitable at these levels, but the FCF that fuels the company’s return to shareholders might become slimmer and slimmer.

Another risk might emanate from the asset quality in the Permian Basin, as Tier 1 acreage is being drilled up, pushing PR to move into Tier 2 zones that could have much lower reserves. If the quality drops, PR will have to drill more wells to just keep its production at the same levels.

Conclusion

To be fair, I believe that PR is the best-run independent E&P on the market, as highlighted by how efficient it is. And while it is not the best time to invest in the O&G industry as a whole, I would still buy PR based on the motto of buying a great company at a good price. I rate Permian Resources as a Strong Buy.

data.title: Allspring California Tax-Free Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Quarterly review

  • The fund underperformed its benchmark, the Bloomberg California Municipal Bond Index, for the three-month period that ended September 30, 2025.
  • Sector and duration/curve positioning were the largest drivers of performance while credit and security selection detracted.

Market review

Municipal returns were positive (3.00%) and outperformed taxable fixed income asset classes, with the Bloomberg Taxable Municipal Bond Index returning 2.51%, Treasuries 1.51%, the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index 2.03%, corporates 2.60%, and U.S. high yield 2.54%. Within municipal credit tiers, A-rated bonds performed best (3.08%) and high yield bonds were the weakest (1.63%). The yield on the U.S. Treasury 10-year note ended September at 4.16%, down 8 basis points (bps; 100 bps equal 1.00%) over the quarter and down 42 bps from the start of 2025. U.S. equity markets were positive for the quarter.

With the downward movement of the municipal curve, shorter-end municipal-to-Treasury ratios decreased during the quarter while longer ratios remained cheap. Municipal 2-year, 5-year, and 10-year ratios of 64%, 62%, and 70%, respectively, were rich, while the 30-year ratio of 90% remained the cheapest portion of the tax-exempt market relative to taxable fixed income.

Net inflows were $11 billion for the quarter, and gross issuance was approximately $157 billion (a 3% quarter-over-quarter decrease), with tax-exempt issuance of $150 billion (a 0.5% quarter-over-quarter increase).

In September, the Federal Open Market Committee lowered its benchmark interest rate to a range of 4.00–4.25%. The municipal benchmark yield curve moved downward, with rates on the short end declining by as much as 36 bps and longer rates by as much as 30 bps. Market expectations favor at least one additional cut this year, though the size and timing of Federal Reserve action remains uncertain. The rate environment reinforces the case for municipal bonds as a stable, income-generating asset amid ongoing market volatility.

Without this cap, the fund's returns would have been lower. The expense ratio paid by an investor is the net expense ratio (the total annual fund operating expenses after fee waivers) as stated in the prospectus.

Portfolio Positioning - Portfolio Attribution* And Strategy

Duration—contributor

  • The fund was long duration relative to the benchmark during the period, which contributed to performance as yields moved lower across the curve.
  • Municipal bond returns were positive for the quarter. We expect record issuance to continue through the end of the year and to outpace fund flows, which points to a weaker technical environment for municipals. Flows remained positive through the quarter, with year-to-date inflows at $29 billion, though total inflows remain below historical averages. We remain bullish on municipals, because absolute rates represent a compelling entry point, and over the near term, we expect income to be the driver for the asset class. With the recent yield curve volatility, we expect to maintain duration closer to neutral, and we see opportunities in the intermediate portion of the curve and expect to be selective about adding duration.

Yield curve—modest contributor

  • Returns were positive across all maturity segments for the benchmark, and longer maturity/duration bonds outperformed shorter maturity/duration bonds by a wide margin. While the fund benefited from an overweight to longer maturity bonds, we do have a larger allocation to shorter calls that reduced the effective maturity relative to the index. As a result, this limited the price appreciation as the market rallied.
  • The municipal benchmark curve shifted downward during the third quarter, with rates falling by 19–36 bps across the curve, and the spread of 186 bps between the 1-year and 30-year Municipal Market Data (MMD) tightened, down from 197 bps at the beginning of the quarter. We remain attentive to pockets of weakness or opportunity that may arise. With only 24 bps between 20-year and 30-year MMD, the continued compression makes extremely long-dated exposure less attractive for investors. In our view, the risk/reward trade-off strongly favors taking a more measured approach to curve allocations.

Quality—detractor

  • Returns were positive across the credit spectrum but were somewhat mixed during the period. AAA and A were the top-performing rating categories, and AA and BBB were the bottom-performing rating categories. The fund remains overweight A/BBB and underweight AAA/AA.
  • Credit spreads remain tight. Despite the rate volatility, the spread between AAA and high yield credits remains near the tightest levels over the past two years, making credit relatively more expensive. Looking ahead, we believe credit fundamentals are well supported by the economy, though the possibility of credit spread widening remains ever present. There are opportunities for more risk-taking investors to explore the high yield space; however, as the July default by Brightline Florida demonstrates, security selection remains crucial in this segment of the market.

Sector—contributor

  • We continue to be underweight general obligation (GO) bonds and overweight revenue bonds. Controlling for duration, curve, and quality allocation, contributors to relative performance included an overweight allocation to the housing sector as well as underweight allocations to the water/sewer and state GO sectors. Detractors included our underweight allocation to the local GO sector and overweight to the special tax sector.
  • In times of economic uncertainty, GO bonds backed by strong income and sales tax receipts, along with essential services provided by municipalities, are generally more defensive than discretionary sectors. We remain focused on these areas while also seeking opportunities in higher-beta revenue sectors where price dislocations create value. These dynamics underscore the need for an experienced team with institutional bandwidth to navigate an evolving market.

Issue selection—detractor

  • Controlling for duration, curve, quality, and sector allocation effects, security selection was negative for the quarter. Relative to the benchmark, the largest contribution effects were seen in the state GO, electric, and industrial development revenue/pollution control revenue sectors. Selection within the local GO, leasing, and education sectors was negative.
  • The volatility observed in the first nine months of the year serves as a reminder to bond investors that municipals are credit products and not just a rates asset class. Security selection remains critical, as certain credits may diverge from the broader market due to forced selling, liquidity constraints, and structural factors. These inefficiencies can lead to atypical execution and present opportunities for strong risk-adjusted returns among liquidity providers. Prudent liquidity management has been a hallmark of our investment process, enabling us to capitalize on ongoing market dislocations while identifying compelling relative value opportunities.

Benchmark Descriptions:

The Bloomberg California Municipal Bond Index is the California component of the Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index, an unmanaged index composed of long-term tax-exempt bonds with a minimum credit rating of Baa. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index is an unmanaged index composed of long-term tax-exempt bonds with a minimum credit rating of Baa. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The Lipper averages are compiled by Lipper, Inc., an independent mutual fund research and rating service. Each Lipper average represents a universe of funds that are similar in investment objective. You cannot invest directly in a Lipper average.

The Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index is a broad-based benchmark that measures the investment-grade, U.S.-dollar-denominated, fixed-rate taxable bond market, including Treasuries, government-related and corporate securities, mortgage-backed securities (agency fixed-rate and hybrid adjustable-rate mortgage pass-throughs), asset-backed securities, and commercial mortgage-backed securities. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The Bloomberg Taxable Municipal Bond Index tracks the performance of U.S. municipal bonds that are not exempt from federal income tax. This index represents a diversified, investment-grade portion of the U.S. municipal bond market, covering issuers such as state and local governments and their agencies. These bonds are distinct from the more common tax-exempt municipal bonds because they are subject to taxation. You cannot invest directly in an index.

Definition of Terms:

30-day SEC yield: The 30-day SEC yield is calculated with a standardized formula mandated by the SEC. The formula is based on maximum offering price per share and includes the effect of any fee waivers. Without waivers, yields would be reduced. The 30-day unsubsidized SEC yield does not reflect waivers in effect. A fund's actual distribution rate will differ from the SEC yield, and any income distributions from the fund may be higher or lower than the SEC yield.

Average maturity: Average maturity represents the weighted average effective maturity. For a portfolio of bonds, average effective maturity is the weighted average of the maturities of the underlying bonds. The maturities are computed by weighting each bond's maturity by its market value with respect to the portfolio and the likelihood of any of the bonds being called. In a pool of mortgages, this would also account for the likelihood of prepayments on the mortgages.

Alpha: measures the difference between a fund's actual returns and its expected performance given its level of risk (as measured by beta).

Beta: measures fund volatility relative to general market movements. It is a standardized measure of systematic risk in comparison with a specified index. The benchmark beta is 1.00 by definition.

Credit-quality ratings: Credit-quality ratings apply to underlying holdings of the fund and not the fund itself. Standard & Poor's and Fitch rate the creditworthiness of bonds from AAA (highest) to D (lowest). Standard & Poor's rates the creditworthiness of short-term notes from SP-1 (highest) to SP-3 (lowest). Ratings from A to CCC may be modified by the addition of a plus (+) or minus (-) sign to show relative standing within the rating categories. Moody's rates the creditworthiness of bonds from Aaa (highest) to CC (lowest). Ratings Aa to B may be modified by the addition of a number 1 (highest) to 3 (lowest) to show relative standing within the ratings categories. Moody's rates the creditworthiness of short-term U.S. tax-exempt municipal securities from MIG-1/VMIG-1 (highest) to SG (lowest). Credit quality and credit-quality ratings are subject to change.

Duration: Duration is a measurement of the sensitivity of a bond's price to changes in Treasury yields. A fund's duration is the weighted average of duration of the bonds in the portfolio. Duration should be interpreted as the approximate change in a bond's (or fund's) price for a 100-basis-point change in Treasury yields. Duration is based on historical performance and does not represent future results.

Yield curve: The yield curve is a graphical representation of fixed-income security yields (usually U.S. Treasuries) at their respective maturities, starting with the shortest time to maturity and sequentially plotting in a line chart to the longest maturity. The yield curve is based on historical performance and does not represent future results.

Risks: All investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principal. Bond values fluctuate in response to the financial condition of individual issuers, general market and economic conditions, and changes in interest rates. Changes in market conditions and government policies may lead to periods of heightened volatility in the bond market and reduced liquidity for certain bonds held by the fund. In general, when interest rates rise, bond values fall and investors may lose principal value. Interest rate changes and their impact on the fund and its share price can be sudden and unpredictable. Municipal securities offered by the state of California and its subdivisions, authorities and corporations are subject to the risk of unfavorable developments occurring in the state, such as the inability of the issuer to repay the obligation, the possibility of future tax and legislative changes and other factors, that may adversely impact the liquidity and value of the municipal securities in which the fund invests. A portion of the fund's income may be subject to federal, state, and/or local income taxes or the alternative minimum tax. Any capital gains distributions may be taxable. Consult the fund's prospectus for additional information on these and other risks.

The Morningstar Rating for funds, or star rating, is calculated for managed products (including mutual funds, variable annuity and variable life subaccounts, exchange-traded funds, closed-end funds, and separate accounts) with at least a three-year history. Exchange-traded funds and open-ended mutual funds are considered a single population for comparative purposes. It is calculated based on a Morningstar risk-adjusted return measure that accounts for variation in a managed product's monthly excess performance, placing more emphasis on downward variations and rewarding consistent performance. The Morningstar Rating does not include any adjustment for sales loads. The top 10% of products in each product category receive 5 stars, the next 22.5% receive 4 stars, the next 35% receive 3 stars, the next 22.5% receive 2 stars, and the bottom 10% receive 1 star. The Overall Morningstar Rating for a managed product is derived from a weighted average of the performance figures associated with its 3-, 5-, and 10-year (if applicable) Morningstar Rating metrics. The weights are: 100% 3-year rating for 36–59 months of total returns, 60% 5-year rating/40% 3-year rating for 60–119 months of total returns, and 50% 10-year rating/30% 5-year rating/20% 3-year rating for 120 or more months of total returns. While the 10-year overall star rating formula seems to give the most weight to the 10-year period, the most recent 3-year period actually has the greatest impact because it is included in all three rating periods.

Across U.S.-domiciled municipal California long funds, the California Tax-Free Fund received 3 stars among 104 funds, 3 stars among 103 funds, and 3 stars among 80 funds for the 3-, 5-, and 10-year periods, respectively. The Morningstar Rating is for the Institutional Class only; other classes may have different performance characteristics.

The Morningstar absolute ranking is based on the fund's total return rank relative to all funds that have the same category for the same time period. Morningstar rankings do not include the effect of sales charges. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

Some of Morningstar's proprietary calculations, including the Morningstar Rating, are not customarily calculated based on adjusted historical returns. However, for new share classes/channels, Morningstar may calculate an extended-performance Morningstar Rating. The extended performance is calculated by adjusting the historical total returns of the oldest share class of a fund to reflect the fee structure of the younger share class/channel, attaching this data to the younger share class's performance record, and then compounding the adjusted plus actual monthly returns into the extended-performance Morningstar risk-adjusted return for the 3-, 5-, and 10-year time periods. The Morningstar risk-adjusted returns are used to determine the extended-performance Morningstar Rating.
